title: Polynomial special products: perfect square
description: Find special products (perfect squares and difference of squares) of "advanced" binomials: binomials with leading coefficients other than 1; binomials with higher degrees; and binomials with two variables.

hints:
We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(4+7a^2\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(4\right)^2+2\left(4\right)\left(7a^2\right)+\left(7a^2\right)^2
\\\\
&=16+56a^2+49a^4
\\\\
&=49a^4+56a^2+16
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(5+6b^3\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(5\right)^2+2\left(5\right)\left(6b^3\right)+\left(6b^3\right)^2
\\\\
&=25+60b^3+36b^6
\\\\
&=36b^6+60b^3+25
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(8x^4+1\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(8x^4\right)^2+2\left(8x^4\right)\left(1\right)+\left(1\right)^2
\\\\
&=64x^8+16x^4+1
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(9y^5+2\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(9y^5\right)^2+2\left(9y^5\right)\left(2\right)+\left(2\right)^2
\\\\
&=81y^{10}+36y^5+4
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Since we have a minus sign, let's rewrite the binomial as a sum where the second term is negative, then use the pattern.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(3-8w^2\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(3+\left(-8w^2\right)\right)^2
\\\\
&=(3)^2+2(3)(-8w^2)+(-8w^2)^2
\\\\
&=9-48w^2+64w^4
\\\\
&=64w^4-48w^2+9
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Since we have a minus sign, let's rewrite the binomial as a sum where the second term is negative, then use the pattern.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(2-7m^6\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(2+\left(-7m^6\right)\right)^2
\\\\
&=(2)^2+2(2)(-7m^6)+(-7m^6)^2
\\\\
&=4-28m^6+49m^{12}
\\\\
&=49m^{12}-28m^6+4
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Since we have a minus sign, let's rewrite the binomial as a sum where the second term is negative, then use the pattern.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(9n^7-1\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(9n^7+\left(-1\right)\right)^2
\\\\
&=(9n^7)^2+2(9n^7)(-1)+(-1)^2
\\\\
&=81n^{14}-18n^7+1
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Since we have a minus sign, let's rewrite the binomial as a sum where the second term is negative, then use the pattern.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(4t^3-5\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(4t^3+\left(-5\right)\right)^2
\\\\
&=(4t^3)^2+2(4t^3)(-5)+(-5)^2
\\\\
&=16t^6-40t^3+25
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(2x^3+4x^5\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(2x^3\right)^2+2\left(2x^3\right)\left(4x^5\right)+\left(4x^5\right)^2
\\\\
&=4x^6+16x^8+16x^{10}
\\\\
&=16x^{10}+16x^8+4x^6
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(6y^2+5y^4\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(6y^2\right)^2+2\left(6y^2\right)\left(5y^4\right)+\left(5y^4\right)^2
\\\\
&=36y^4+60y^6+25y^8
\\\\
&=25y^8+60y^6+36y^4
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(3z^5+7z^2\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(3z^5\right)^2+2\left(3z^5\right)\left(7z^2\right)+\left(7z^2\right)^2
\\\\
&=9z^{10}+42z^7+49z^4
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(8w^4+w^3\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(8w^4\right)^2+2\left(8w^4\right)\left(w^3\right)+\left(w^3\right)^2
\\\\
&=64w^8+16w^7+w^6
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Since we have a minus sign, let's rewrite the binomial as a sum where the second term is negative, then use the pattern.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(3c^4-5c^6\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(3c^4+\left(-5c^6\right)\right)^2
\\\\
&=(3c^4)^2+2(3c^4)(-5c^6)+(-5c^6)^2
\\\\
&=9c^8-30c^{10}+25c^{12}
\\\\
&=25c^{12}-30c^{10}+9c^8
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Since we have a minus sign, let's rewrite the binomial as a sum where the second term is negative, then use the pattern.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(4d^2-2d^7\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(4d^2+\left(-2d^7\right)\right)^2
\\\\
&=(4d^2)^2+2(4d^2)(-2d^7)+(-2d^7)^2
\\\\
&=16d^4-16d^9+4d^{14}
\\\\
&=4d^{14}-16d^9+16d^4
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Since we have a minus sign, let's rewrite the binomial as a sum where the second term is negative, then use the pattern.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(2a^6-6a^3\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(2a^6+\left(-6a^3\right)\right)^2
\\\\
&=(2a^6)^2+2(2a^6)(-6a^3)+(-6a^3)^2
\\\\
&=4a^{12}-24a^9+36a^6
\end{align}$

We can expand this expression using the "perfect square" pattern (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)^2=P^2+2PQ+Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Since we have a minus sign, let's rewrite the binomial as a sum where the second term is negative, then use the pattern.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left(7b^5-b^2\right)^2
\\\\
&=\left(7b^5+\left(-b^2\right)\right)^2
\\\\
&=(7b^5)^2+2(7b^5)(-b^2)+(-b^2)^2
\\\\
&=49b^{10}-14b^7+b^4
\end{align}$
